coredata.dc:title: Impact of varying lateral interface ratio on the excitonic and spectral features of planar Graphene Quantum Dot/h-BN van der Waals heterostructures for optoelectronic applications: A Density Functional theory study
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Basak, Tista
Basak, Tushima
Roondhe, Vaishali
coredata.dc:description: A critical analysis of the lateral atomically thin heterointerfaces in planar van der Waals heterostructures (vdWhs) synthesized by embedding graphene quantum dot (GQD) in hexagonal boron nitride (h-BN) matrix is extremely crucial to comprehend the optical response of these systems. Here, we propose a unique technique of varying the percentage of heterojunction boundaries to effectively modulate the electronic and optical characteristics of different high-symmetrically aligned planar GQD/h-BN bilayer vdWhs with time-dependent density functional theory-based computations. The incorporation of GQD in the h-BN bilayers decreases the electronic and optical bandgap with the reduction being more pronounced for the optical gap. Enhancing the proportion of these heterointerfaces leads to switching of the lowest energy exciton determining the optical gap from intralayer to long-lived interlayer type. Markedly, the confinement potential at the GQD location strongly localizes these excitons, heralding a potential route of tuning the optical gap by regulating the size/geometry of embedded GQDs. The most intense optical peak energy is altered by a greater extent than the optical gap by varying the ratio of heterojunctions. We finally prove that heterointerface engineering modulates the type of band alignment, range and nature of entire optical profile, essential for the design of future optoelectronic devices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: van der Waals heterostructures
Hexagonal boron nitride
Graphene quantum dots
Optical properties
Time-dependent density functional theory

xocs:item-toc-section-title.ce:italic: Structural energetics, electronic bandgap, optical gap and excitonic binding energy

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Optimized ground-state geometries of (a) AA-h-BN, (b) AA-h-BN-1GQD, (c) AA-h-BN-2GQD, (d) AB-h-BN, (e) AB-h-BN-1GQD and (f) AB-h-BN-2GQD. The nitrogen, boron, carbon and hydrogen atoms are represented by blue, pink, gray and white spheres, respectively. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Energy-level diagram illustrating the frontier molecular orbitals (H and L) of pristine h-BN bilayers and planar GQD/h-BN bilayer vdWhs. The solid and dashed black (blue) lines represent the H and L levels of pristine AA-h-BN (AB-h-BN) structures, respectively. The pink solid and dashed lines denote the H and L levels of AA stacked planar GQD/h-BN vdWhs, namely AA-h-BN-1GQD and AA-h-BN-2GQD (designated as 1GQD and 2GQD), respectively, while the red solid and dashed lines denote the H and L levels of AB stacked planar GQD/h-BN vdWhs, namely AB-h-BN-1GQD and AB-h-BN-2GQD (denoted as 1GQD and 2GQD), respectively. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Total density of states (TDOS) and partial density of states (PDOS) of the pristine h-BN bilayers.

Total density of states (TDOS) and partial density of states (PDOS) of the planar GQD/h-BN bilayer vdWhs. The red, blue and pink solid and dashed lines represent the PDOS of C, B and N in layers 1 and 2, respectively. The TDOS and PDOS are broadened with a uniform Gaussian width of 0.01 a.u. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Partial density of states (PDOS) of carbon atoms near the valence band maximum and conduction band minimum for (a) AA-h-BN-1GQD, (b) AA-h-BN-2GQD, (c) AB-h-BN-1GQD, (d) AB-h-BN-2GQD, respectively. The TDOS and PDOS are broadened with a uniform Gaussian width of 0.01 a.u.
Optical absorption profile of pristine h-BN bilayers and planar GQD/h-BN bilayer vdWhs, derived from the TDDFT approach.

Electron–hole distribution maps of the first and high intensity (HI) optical peaks of (a, b, c) AA-h-BN-1GQD, and (d, e, f) AB-h-BN-1GQD, respectively, where green and blue depicts electrons and holes, respectively. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Electron–hole distribution maps of the first and high intensity (HI) optical peaks of (a, b, c, d) AA-h-BN-2GQD, and (e, f, g, h) AB-h-BN-2GQD, respectively, where green and blue depicts electrons and holes, respectively. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Electronic bandgap, optical gap and excitonic binding energy of the pristine h-BN bilayers and planar GQD/h-BN bilayer vdWhs.

head.ce:title: Impact of varying lateral interface ratio on the excitonic and spectral features of planar Graphene Quantum Dot/h-BN van der Waals heterostructures for optoelectronic applications: A Density Functional theory study

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Indian Institute of Technology Bombay, Powai, Mumbai 400076, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A critical analysis of the lateral atomically thin heterointerfaces in planar van der Waals heterostructures (vdWhs) synthesized by embedding graphene quantum dot (GQD) in hexagonal boron nitride (h-BN) matrix is extremely crucial to comprehend the optical response of these systems. Here, we propose a unique technique of varying the percentage of heterojunction boundaries to effectively modulate the electronic and optical characteristics of different high-symmetrically aligned planar GQD/h-BN bilayer vdWhs with time-dependent density functional theory-based computations. The incorporation of GQD in the h-BN bilayers decreases the electronic and optical bandgap with the reduction being more pronounced for the optical gap. Enhancing the proportion of these heterointerfaces leads to switching of the lowest energy exciton determining the optical gap from intralayer to long-lived interlayer type. Markedly, the confinement potential at the GQD location strongly localizes these excitons, heralding a potential route of tuning the optical gap by regulating the size/geometry of embedded GQDs. The most intense optical peak energy is altered by a greater extent than the optical gap by varying the ratio of heterojunctions. We finally prove that heterointerface engineering modulates the type of band alignment, range and nature of entire optical profile, essential for the design of future optoelectronic devices.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The advent of atomically thin two-dimensional (2D) materials has opened up a new research frontier in the past few decades as they exhibit drastically distinct electronic and optical properties from their bulk counterparts. The interfacing of two different 2D materials with similar lattice structure by weak van der Waals interlayer coupling to form van der Waals heterostructure (vdWh) can further enrich the tunability range of the individual component attributes [1]. In recent years, van der Waals heterostructures comprising of hexagonal boron nitride (h-BN) and graphene have sparked tremendous research interest due to their very high structural similarity (lattice mismatch < 1.7%) [2] and disparate electronic properties; h-BN has a large and indirect bandgap while graphene is a zero bandgap semimetal. This variation in the bandgap can significantly modulate the light–matter interaction in h-BN/graphene vdWh leading to efficient light absorption or emission over a broad energy band, thus, providing a promising platform for designing high-performance optoelectronic and photonic devices with adjustable properties [3,4].

Existing experimental and theoretical studies have extensively focussed on the impact of layer stacking sequences, interlayer distances, twist angles and lateral displacements in deciding the electronic and optical features of vertically stacked h-BN/graphene vdWhs [2,5–12]. The quantum confinement and reduced dielectric screening in the out-of-plane direction of h-BN/graphene vdWh enhances the Coulomb interactions, giving rise to the formation of strongly bound electron–hole pairs (excitons) which play a significant role in governing the optical response of the heterostructure. The nature of the excitonic species such as intralayer exciton and interlayer exciton depends on the stacking sequence, electronic band-structure and presence of defects in these van der Waals heterostructures. However, very few reports exist on the optical characterization of planar or in-plane van der Waals heterostructures synthesized by embedding graphene quantum dot (GQD) in h-BN matrix (GQD/h-BN) [13,14]. Recently, Kim et al. [13] fabricated vdWh by vertically stacking four layers of in-plane GQD/h-BN films and investigated their photoluminescence (PL) spectra. Their experimental studies demonstrate that the localized energy states at the heterojunction between h-BN and graphene gives rise to blue-light emission which can be used for designing LEDs, indicating that interface boundaries are a key factor in regulating the optical properties. However, the critical impact of increasing atomically-thin heterojunction boundaries on the electronic, excitonic and optical characteristics of vertically stacked layers of planar GQD/h-BN vdWhs is not yet explored in detail.

To circumvent this problem, we employ time-dependent density functional theory to probe the electronic and spectral features of bilayer vdWh composed of either pristine h-BN layers or planar GQD/h-BN films having varying proportion of GQD and h-BN boundaries. We initially consider the simplest high-symmetry AA stacking sequence of pristine h-BN bilayer (AA-h-BN), where B and N of the top layer are exactly aligned with the B and N atoms of the bottom layer [15–17]. The planar GQD/h-BN vdWhs with increasing percentage of heterojunctions are then constructed by (i) embedding GQD in one of the layers of AA stacked h-BN, i.e., (AA-h-BN-1GQD) and (ii) incorporating GQD in both the layers of AA stacked h-BN, i.e., (AA-h-BN-2GQD). Subsequently, we explore the interplay between the stacking order and rising GQD/h-BN interface ratio in tuning the electronic and optical properties by first investigating a different type of stacking, namely, the stable AB configuration of pristine h-BN bilayer (AB-h-BN), obtained from the AA phase by the translational sliding of the top layer with respect to the bottom layer by an inter-atomic distance of 1.40 Å [15–17], followed by an analysis of their planar heterostructures designed by introducing GQD in either one or both the layers of AB stacked h-BN, henceforth, designated as AB-h-BN-1GQD and AB-h-BN-2GQD, respectively. We identify that the character of the lowest energy exciton governing the optical gap can be varied from intralayer to interlayer type with longer lifetime, crucial for quantum technology applications and light emitting devices, by amplifying the extent of lateral heterointerfaces. In particular, these excitons are primarily trapped in the domain of the GQDs, signifying that controlling the confinement potential by altering the size/geometry of embedded GQDs can selectively modulate the optical gap. Finally, a detailed investigation of the electronic characteristics and the spectral profile of these planar GQD/h-BN bilayer vdWhs highlights that tuning the proportion of heterojunction edges is extremely critical in deciding the binding energies of the excitonic species, type of band alignment, electronic and optical bandgap, range of optical absorption, position/oscillator strengths of the optical peaks and charge-transfer character of the excitations, thus, pre-determining their prospective functionalities in the design of tunable optoelectronic and photonic devices.

The optimized ground-state geometries of all the pristine h-BN bilayers and planar GQD/h-BN bilayer vdWhs (Fig. 1a–f) are obtained from the Gaussian 16 program package [18] by employing the 6-31G(d,p) basis set and the hybrid B3LYP functional [19–21]. The weak van der Waals interactions between the atoms are taken into account by applying the Grimme’s dispersion correction to the hybrid B3LYP functional. In the structures optimized at the density functional theory (DFT) level, the bond-lengths between B and N varies between 1.43–1.45 Å while that between CC atoms in the GQD domain ranges from 1.41–1.43 Å . However, at the interface boundary between the GQD and h-BN domains, the bond-lengths between C–B atoms is elongated slightly to 1.53 Å while that between C and N remains close to 1.41 Å , implying that the interaction between C and N atoms are stronger than C and B atoms.

The linear optical absorption spectra of the optimized systems are then computed by implementing the time-dependent density functional theory (TDDFT) approach. The total density of states (TDOS), partial density of states (PDOS), electron–hole distribution maps and charge-transfer length
△ r
of these vdWhs are determined by the Multiwfn code [22]. The charge-transfer length [23] is analyzed to decide quantitatively the charge-transfer character of the excited states of these planar GQD/h-BN bilayer vdWhs.

ce:section-title.ce:italic: Structural energetics, electronic bandgap, optical gap and excitonic binding energy

ce:section.ce:para: A detailed comparison of the structural energetics of the optimized pure h-BN bilayers indicates that the AB stacked h-BN is more stable than AA-h-BN by 0.03 eV. The incorporation of GQD in one of the layers of AA or AB stacked h-BN increases the total energy of the system by 3.14 eV and this increment is doubled by the inclusion of GQD in both the layers of these systems, implying that planar GQD/h-BN bilayer vdWhs are less stable than pristine h-BN bilayers.

Table 1 presents the electronic bandgap, optical gap and excitonic binding energy of the pristine h-BN bilayers and planar GQD/h-BN bilayer vdWhs considered in this work. Our computed values of electronic bandgap for AA-h-BN and AB-h-BN are in close agreement with the previous theoretical DFT results [15], enclosed in square brackets. Earlier experimental measurements for monolayer h-BN have reported the value of optical bandgap as 5.85 eV [24]. This coupled with earlier theoretical predictions that number of layers and stacking sequence can modulate the optical gap validates our computed results for pristine AA and AB bilayer h-BN. Moreover, our calculated excitonic binding energy for pristine AA and AB stacked h-BN are in excellent agreement with the earlier experimental value of 0.15 eV [25] for bulk h-BN, lending credibility to our predictions. Our results reveal that the incorporation of GQD in h-BN bilayers decreases both the electronic and optical bandgap with the reduction being more pronounced for the optical gap. In particular, this decrease in the electronic and optical bandgap is more for GQD embedded in AB stacked h-BN as compared to GQD in AA stacked h-BN bilayers. The difference between the electronic and optical gap, which is equal to the excitonic binding energy, becomes more discernible as the boundary region between GQD and h-BN domains is increased by introducing GQD in both the layers of h-BN. Hence, the small excitonic binding energy of pristine h-BN bilayers can be substantially enhanced by the incorporation of GQDs, rendering the planar GQD/h-BN bilayer vdWhs as potential candidates for designing light-emitting devices.

A comprehensive analysis of the variation in the energies of the highest occupied molecular orbital (H) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (L) of the planar GQD/h-BN bilayer vdWhs with respect to the pristine h-BN bilayers (Fig. 2) reveals that the incorporation of GQD increases/decreases the energy of the H/L levels due to the introduction of additional energy levels of carbon atoms, resulting in an overall decrease in the electronic bandgap. Specifically, the highest occupied molecular orbital energy shift is more discernible than the L level and the magnitude of this H level shift can be significantly enhanced by incrementing the percentage of interface region between GQD and h-BN domains. Moreover, the extent of change in the energy of H/L levels of the planar GQD/h-BN bilayer vdWhs with respect to the pristine h-BN bilayers is more pronounced for AB stacked vdWh configurations while the augmentation in the proportion of heterojunctions is more instrumental in modulating the energy of the H level for vdWhs with AA alignment.
Hence, our results signify that both the heterointerface ratio and nature of stacking sequence play a decisive role in tuning the electronic bandgap of planar GQD/h-BN bilayer vdWhs.

The intricate role of embedded GQD in altering the energy band-structure of h-BN bilayers is now probed by examining the total density of states (TDOS) and partial density of states (PDOS) of the pristine h-BN bilayers [Fig. 3a–b] and planar GQD/h-BN bilayer vdWhs [Fig. 4a–d].

Our results reveal that for pure AA and AB stacked h-BN bilayers, the N atoms primarily contribute to the states near the valence band maximum (VBM) while the conduction band minimum (CBM) of the TDOS is decided by the B atoms. The implantation of GQD in the h-BN matrix introduces additional states near the VBM and CBM and their location is primarily ascertained by the carbon atoms. In particular, the introduction of GQD in one of the layers of h-BN (AA-h-BN-1GQD or AB-h-BN-1GQD) results in Type I band alignment [28] wherein the CBM and VBM are due to the carbon atoms in the same layer while the inclusion of GQD in both the h-BN layers (AA-h-BN-2GQD or AB-h-BN-2GQD) gives rise to Type II band alignment [28] wherein CBM and VBM are decided by the carbon atoms embedded in different layers. The Type II band alignment in AA-h-BN-2GQD and AB-h-BN-2GQD makes these planar GQD/h-BN bilayer vdWhs ideal candidates to host spatially separated electron–hole pairs i.e., interlayer excitons with long lifetimes.

A comprehensive analysis of the PDOS of AA-h-BN-1GQD or AB-h-BN-1GQD structures [Fig. 5a–d] shows that their VBM and CBM can be predominantly ascribed to the interfacial carbon atoms bonded to the boron and nitrogen atoms, respectively. In case of AA-h-BN-2GQD or AB-h-BN-2GQD configurations, the VBM is mainly localized on the carbon atoms attached to the boron atoms in one layer while the C atoms bonded to the N atoms in the other layer chiefly contribute to the CBM. Hence, our results highlight that different types of band alignment in planar GQD/h-BN bilayer vdWhs can be realized by enhancing the percentage of heterojunctions.

We now focus on the impact of implanting GQD and enhancing its density in determining the optical response of h-BN bilayers by analyzing their linear absorption spectra derived from the TDDFT approach (Fig. 6a–f) and examining the quantitative information of the corresponding excited states (Tables S1- S6) [29]. The dominant excitonic effects in bilayer vdWh due to reduced Coulomb screening play a critical role in deciding their optical response and therefore, accurate ab initio computational methods incorporating electron-correlation effects are essential in capturing their optical characteristics. To authenticate the accuracy of our computations, we compare the energies of the absorption peaks of pristine AA- and AB-stacked h-BN bilayer with the earlier experimental absorption spectra of pure h-BN single crystals [25]. The excellent agreement of the experimental fundamental absorption spectra (in the range 5.822 to 5.968 eV) with the high-intensity peak region of AA- and AB-stacked h-BN bilayer (5.78–5.92 eV and 5.82–5.92 eV, respectively) validates the precision of our results.

Our results highlight that the entire optical profile of planar GQD/h-BN bilayer vdWhs is significantly red-shifted in comparison to the absorption spectra of the pristine h-BN bilayers. A greater interface region between GQD and h-BN initiated by the introduction of GQD in both the layers of h-BN increases the overall red-shift, with the magnitude of the shift being more discernible at the high energy range. In particular, the absorption spectrum of planar GQD/h-BN bilayer vdWhs extends over a broad span of energies, ranging from visible to UV domain. This is in stark contrast to the narrow span of UV energies absorbed by the pure h-BN bilayers. The low energy peaks in the visible and near-UV band of the heterostructures are derived from transitions between the electronic energy levels of the GQD while the optical excitations in the high energy mid-UV region around 4 eV originates from transitions involving the energy levels of h-BN and GQD. Consequently, the characteristics of the high energy optical spectra are strongly impacted by the alignment nature of the planar GQD/h-BN bilayer vdWhs. The high intensity peaks are in the high energy range for pure h-BN bilayers, while these peaks are in the low energy region for systems having GQD in one or both of the h-BN layers. The high oscillator strength of the first optical peak of AA-h-BN-1GQD or AB-h-BN-1GQD is drastically reduced by the inclusion of GQD in both the layers of h-BN (AA-h-BN-2GQD or AB-h-BN-2GQD). The first peak of the pristine h-BN bilayers and planar GQD/h-BN bilayer vdWhs is due to the dominant contribution of a single electron transition from the H to the L energy level (optical gap). The magnitude of red-shift of this peak is enhanced as the percentage of heterojunction between GQD and h-BN domain is increased. The strength of the most intense (MI) peak of GQD/h-BN vdWhs increases for higher ratio of GQD and h-BN boundary. The MI peak of AA-h-BN-2GQD/AB-h-BN-2GQD is blue-/red-shifted with respect to AA-h-BN-1GQD/AB-h-BN-1GQD configurations. Markedly, the magnitude of this shift exhibited by the MI peak is more significant for AB-stacked GQD/h-BN vdWhs as compared to AA-stacked GQD/h-BN systems. Hence, the nature and magnitude of shift exhibited by the MI peak in systems with greater proportion of GQD is notably affected by the specific arrangement of the h-BN bilayers. Moreover, our results demonstrate that the variance in MI peak energies attained by tuning the percentage of GQD/h-BN heterojunctions is more than their optical gap deviation.

An analysis of the electron–hole distribution maps of the first and high intensity optical peaks of planar GQD/h-BN bilayer vdWhs [Fig. 7a–f] clearly indicates that the first peak of AA-h-BN-1GQD or AB-h-BN-1GQD is due to an intralayer exciton, with the electrons and holes residing in the same layer. On the other hand, the first peak of AA-h-BN-2GQD or AB-h-BN-2GQD [Fig. 8a–h] is due to an interlayer exciton, comprising of electrons in one layer and holes in the other layer. This emphasizes that increasing the percentage of heterojunctions transforms the nature of the lowest-energy exciton deciding the optical gap from intralayer to long-lived interlayer type which is extremely crucial in the domain of quantum technology and light-emitting devices. Specifically, these intralayer/interlayer exciton is strictly localized in the region of the GQDs, signaling that tuning the confinement potential established in the GQD domain by modulating its geometry or size can selectively alter the energy of the intralayer/interlayer exciton giving rise to the optical gap. The spatially localized character of the intralayer exciton enhances the intensity of the first absorption peak of AA-h-BN-1GQD or AB-h-BN-1GQD while the spatially separated feature of the interlayer exciton is responsible for the extremely weak oscillator strength of the first optical peak of AA-h-BN-2GQD or AB-h-BN-2GQD configurations. The high intensity peaks of AA-h-BN-1GQD or AB-h-BN-1GQD are strictly due to intralayer excitons. On the other hand, both intralayer and interlayer excitons contribute to the highly intense peaks of AA-h-BN-2GQD or AB-h-BN-2GQD configurations. Especially, the most intense peak of AA-h-BN-2GQD and the high intensity peak XIII of AB-h-BN-2GQD system is primarily due to an interlayer exciton. This emphasizes that the interlayer excitons can enhance the oscillator strength of the optical peaks originating from excitations between the energy levels of h-BN and GQDs while it decreases the oscillator strength of the low energy absorption peaks involving transitions between the electronic energy levels of GQDs only.

The excitons responsible for the optical excitations can exhibit either localized
△ r < 2
A
̊
or charge-transfer
△ r > 2
A
̊
[30] character (Tables S1- S6) [29]. The excitations of pure h-BN bilayers are identified as purely charge-transfer (CT) states. The implantation of GQD in the h-BN bilayers drastically reduces the charge-transfer behavior of the low energy excited states in the visible and the near-UV range of the vdWhs. This reduction is more evident for AA-h-BN-1GQD or AB-h-BN-1GQD as compared to AA-h-BN-2GQD or AB-h-BN-2GQD configurations. However, the first optical peak of AA-h-BN-2GQD or AB-h-BN-2GQD have significant charge-transfer character and subsequently these charge carriers can be easily separated as compared to the strongly localized excitons responsible for the first optical peak of AA-h-BN-1GQD or AB-h-BN-1GQD systems. Hence, the charge-transfer character of the excited states is determined by the density of the GQD/h-BN heterojunctions.

In summary, we have probed in detail the impact of increasing lateral heterojunction boundaries in determining the electronic and optical attributes of different high-symmetrically stacked planar GQD/h-BN bilayer van der Waals heterostructures with time-dependent density functional theory-based computations. The implantation of GQD in the h-BN bilayers decreases both the electronic and optical bandgap with the reduction being more conspicuous for the optical gap. The enhancement in the proportion of heterojunctions regulates the energy of the highest occupied molecular orbital level by a greater extent as compared to the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital level and this behavior is more pronounced for AA-stacked planar GQD/h-BN bilayer vdWhs, signaling that both the heterointerface ratio and type of stacking arrangement are instrumental in deciding the electronic bandgap of planar GQD/h-BN bilayer vdWhs. We detect that the nature of the lowest energy exciton responsible for the optical gap can be switched from intralayer to long-lived interlayer type, essential for the design of light emitting devices and quantum technology applications, by augmenting the ratio of heterointerfaces. Our results highlight that these excitons are strongly trapped by the confinement potential created at the site of the GQDs, establishing a promising route of tuning the optical gap by tailoring the size/geometry of embedded GQDs. The binding energy of these excitons can be substantially enhanced by incrementing the percentage of GQD/h-BN boundaries. The band alignment in planar GQD/h-BN bilayer vdWhs can also be altered from Type I to Type II by enhancing the percentage of heterojunctions. We observe that the dominant red-shift of the optical absorption spectra triggered by the incorporation of GQD can be magnified by increasing the density of graphene quantum dot arrays. Notably, the energy of the most intense peak can be tuned by a greater extent than the optical gap by varying the ratio of GQD/h-BN interface and depends on the specific arrangement of the h-BN bilayers. For systems with greater proportion of GQD/h-BN interface, the interlayer excitons can enhance the oscillator strength of the optical peaks arising from excitations between the energy levels of h-BN and GQDs while it decreases the oscillator strength of the low energy absorption peaks involving transitions between the electronic energy levels of GQDs only. Finally, our results reveal that the charge-transfer character of the optical excitations decided by the different types of the excitons are dictated by the varying ratio of heterointerfaces. In conclusion, our findings provide a comprehensive insight of the crucial role of heterojunctions in controlling the electronic, excitonic and optical properties of planar GQD/h-BN bilayer vdWhs, creating new avenues in the design of future generation optoelectronic devices.
Tista Basak: Writing – original draft, Supervision, Software, Methodology, Conceptualization. Tushima Basak: Writing – review & editing, Software, Methodology. Vaishali Roondhe: Software.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.mtcomm.2024.109270.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary material related to this article.
MMC S1
Energies, dominant configurations and charge transfer length of the excited states contributing to absorption spectra.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. K. Geim, I. V. Grigorieva, Van der Waals heterostructures, Nature 499 (2013) 419–425.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Structural, electronic, and optical properties of periodic graphene/h-BN van der Waals heterostructures

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W. Aggoune, C. Cocchi, D. Nabok, K. Rezouali, M. A. Belkhir, C. Draxl, Structural, electronic, and optical properties of periodic graphene/h-BN van der Waals heterostructures, Phys. Rev. Mater. 4 (2020) 084001.
